1: zĕkōr-yhwh lĕdāwid ʾēt kol-ʿunnôtô — "O LORD, remember David — remember all his hardships" — zĕkōr (remember, Qal imperative) — divine remembering always entails divine action (cf. Genesis 8:1, Exodus 2:24). The prayer is for YHWH to act based on David's afflictions. ʿunnôtô (his afflictions, hardships, from ʿānāh, to be humbled, afflicted, bowed down) — the same root as the verb for Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exodus 1:11-12). David's devotion to the ark, and his "affliction" in pursuing YHWH's house, is presented as the grounds for YHWH's ongoing faithfulness to the Davidic dynasty.
2: ʾăšer nišbaʿ layhwāh nādar lāʾăbîr yaʿăqōb — "how he made a solemn oath to the LORD and vowed to the Mighty One of Jacob" — ʾăbîr yaʿăqōb (the Mighty One of Jacob) — a rare divine title appearing in Genesis 49:24 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph), Isaiah 49:26, Psalms 60:16 — it emphasizes YHWH's power/might (ʾabbîr = bull-strength, power). The title connects the Davidic covenant to the Abrahamic-Jacobite patriarchal promise.
3: ʾim-ʾābōʾ bĕʾōhel bêtî ʾim-ʾeʿeleh ʿal-ʿereś yĕṣûʿāy — "I will not enter my house, nor climb into my bed" — the vow is structured as two negative conditionals: ʾim-ʾābōʾ (if I enter... [I am forsworn]) — the conditional oath formula. David vowed not to allow himself the comfort of his own dwelling until he had secured a dwelling for YHWH. The historical reference is to David's desire to build the temple (2 Samuel 7:1-3), though the ark-finding narrative in vv6 suggests a different specific moment (2 Sam 6 or 1 Chr 13).
4: ʾim-ʾettēn šĕnat lĕʿênāy lĕʿapʿappay tĕnûmāh — "I will allow no sleep to my eyes, no slumber to my eyelids" — ʿapʿappayim (eyelids, from ʿûp, to fly, flutter — the fluttering movement of eyelids) — a vivid anatomical term for the eyelids. The sleep deprivation vow (no sleep to eyes, no slumber to eyelids) reflects the urgency of the task. Ironically, YHWH is the one who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Psalms 121:3-4) — David's vow imitates divine vigilance for the sake of YHWH's dwelling.
5: ʿad-ʾemṣāʾ māqôm layhwāh mišĕkānôt lāʾăbîr yaʿăqōb — "until I find a fitting place for the LORD — a dwelling for the Mighty One of Jacob" — māqôm (place, site) + mišĕkānôt (dwelling places, tabernacles, from šākan, to dwell, settle) — the repeated word-family behind the tabernacle/Shekinah concept. David's goal is a permanent māqôm for YHWH to settle (šākan), replacing the mobile tent of the wilderness. The title ʾăbîr yaʿăqōb (Mighty One of Jacob) appears for the second time in two verses — the structural echo stresses the Abrahamic-Davidic continuity.
6: hinnēh-šĕmaʿănûhā bĕʾeprātāh mĕṣāʾnûhā biśdê-yāʿar — "we heard of the ark at Ephrathah; we discovered it in the fields of Jaar" — Ephrathah = Bethlehem/its region (Genesis 35:19; Micah 5:2) — the ark was associated with Kiriath-jearim (= Jaar, city of forests), where it had rested for 20 years after being returned from the Philistines (1 Samuel 7:1-2). The "hearing" and "finding" suggest a community knowledge of where the ark was before the grand procession of 2 Sam 6. biśdê-yāʿar (in the fields of Jaar) — the wooded region around Kiriath-jearim.
7: nābôʾāh lĕmišĕkĕnôtāyw niştaḥăweh lahadōm raglāyw — "let us go to his dwelling place; let us bow down at his footstool" — mišĕkĕnôtāyw (his dwelling places) — YHWH's dwelling, using the šākan root again. hadōm raglāyw (his footstool) — the ark was described as YHWH's footstool (1 Chronicles 28:2; Psalms 99:5), the place where the divine feet "rested" on earth. Prostration before the footstool is prostration before the King on the throne above.
8: qûmāh yhwh limnûḥātĕkā ʾattāh wĕʾărôn ʿuzzĕkā — "arise, O LORD, to your resting place — you and the ark that holds your power" — qûmāh (arise, Qal imperative of qûm) — the command to YHWH to arise echoes the ancient procession formula of Numbers 10:35 ("arise, O LORD, and let your enemies be scattered"). limnûḥātĕkā (to your resting place, from nûaḥ, to rest) — the Solomonic temple is envisioned as YHWH's permanent rest after the wandering years of the ark. ʾărôn ʿuzzĕkā (the ark of your strength/power) — not the ark of the covenant formula here but the ark of YHWH's ʿōz (might, power).
9: kōhănêkā yilbĕšû-ṣedeq waḥăsîdêkā yĕrannēnû — "clothe your priests with righteousness, and let your devoted people shout for joy" — yilbĕšû-ṣedeq (may they be clothed with righteousness) — righteousness as a garment (cf. Isaiah 61:10; Job 29:14). The priests clothed with righteousness corresponds to the ark clothed with divine power (v8). ḥăsîdêkā (your devoted ones, your faithful ones, from ḥesed) — those who embody the covenant loyalty that YHWH has shown them. yĕrannēnû (let them shout for joy, from rānan, to cry aloud, shout with joy).
10: baʿăbûr dāwid ʿabdĕkā ʾal-tāšēb pĕnê mĕšîḥĕkā — "for your servant David's sake, do not reject your anointed king" — baʿăbûr (for the sake of, because of) — the Davidic covenant as the ground of the prayer. mĕšîḥĕkā (your anointed, your Messiah) — the term that will carry the full weight of Messianic expectation; here it refers to the reigning Davidic king but within the covenant structure that points forward. The prayer not to "turn away the face" (lōʾ tāšēb pĕnê) of the anointed is a royal intercession.
11: nišbaʿ yhwh lĕdāwid ʾĕmet lōʾ-yāšûb mimmennāh mippĕrî biṭnĕkā ʾāšît lĕkissēʾ-lāk — "the LORD made a true sworn covenant with David — he will never go back on it: 'I will place one of your own descendants upon your throne'" — ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness) — the oath is characterized by ʾĕmet; this is not a conditional covenant but a sworn unilateral commitment. lōʾ-yāšûb mimmennāh (he will not turn back from it) — the divine oath is irrevocable. mippĕrî biṭnĕkā (from the fruit of your body) — the physical Davidic line as the channel of the covenant promise (2 Samuel 7:12-13; 16).
12: ʾim-yišmĕrû bānêkā bĕrîtî wĕʿēdōtî zō ʾălammdēm gam-bĕnêhem ʿădê-ʿad yēšĕbû lĕkissēʾ-lāk — "if your sons keep my covenant and the decrees I teach them, their sons too shall sit upon your throne forever" — this conditional element (vv11b-12) appears to place conditions on the Davidic covenant, in tension with v11a's unconditional promise. The tension runs throughout 2 Sam 7 and Ps 89: YHWH will not abandon the Davidic line even when individual kings sin, but the dynasty's practical flourishing depends on covenant faithfulness. The NT resolution: the Messiah who perfectly keeps the covenant enables the permanent throne.
13: kî-bāḥar yhwh bĕṣiyyôn ʾiwwāh lĕmôšāb lô — "the LORD has chosen Zion; he has made it his desired home" — bāḥar (to choose, elect) — the divine election of Zion parallels the divine election of David; both are unconditional acts of divine choice. ʾiwwāh lĕmôšāb lô (he desired it as a dwelling for himself) — ʾāwāh (to desire, long for, crave) — the language of passionate divine desire: YHWH longed for Zion as a home. This mutual longing (David for YHWH's ark, vv3-5; YHWH for Zion, v13) is the relational core of the Davidic covenant.
14: zōʾt-mĕnûḥātî ʿădê-ʿad pōh-ʾēšēb kî ʾiwtîhāh — "'This is my resting place forever; here I will live, for I have longed for it'" — YHWH speaks directly for the first time in the psalm. mĕnûḥātî ʿădê-ʿad (my resting place unto eternity) — the permanent rest after the wandering of the wilderness and the period of the judges. pōh-ʾēšēb (here I will dwell/sit) — the divine declaration of permanent habitation. The echo of v8's limnûḥātĕkā (to your resting place) closes the circle: what the congregation prayed for (v8), YHWH now declares as his own settled intention (v14).
15: ṣêdāh bārēk ʾăbārēk ʾebyônêhā ʾaśbîaʿ lāḥem — "I will richly bless her food supply; I will fill the poor with bread" — the divine promises flow from YHWH's choice of Zion. ṣêdāh bārēk ʾăbārēk (her provision I will bless abundantly, infinitive absolute construction) — the doubling of the verb (bārēk ʾăbārēk) for intensification: I will truly/abundantly bless. ʾebyônêhā ʾaśbîaʿ lāḥem (her poor I will satisfy with bread) — the social justice dimension of Zion's blessing: YHWH's presence ensures that the poor are fed (Psalms 146:7; Luke 1:53 in the Magnificat).
16: wĕkōhănêhā ʾalbiš yĕšaʿ waḥăsîdêhā rannēn yĕrannēnû — "I will clothe her priests with salvation, and her devoted people will shout with joy" — the divine speech answers v9's prayer: there the congregation prayed for priests clothed with righteousness and faithful people shouting; here YHWH promises priests clothed with yēšaʿ (salvation, deliverance). The exchange: human petition (v9) → divine promise (v16). yēšaʿ (salvation) in place of v9's ṣedeq (righteousness) — salvation as the fulfillment of righteousness.
17: šām ʾaṣmîaḥ qeren lĕdāwid ʿāraktî nēr limsîḥî — "there I will make a shoot of strength grow for David; I have set a lamp ready for my anointed" — ʾaṣmîaḥ qeren (I will cause a horn to sprout/grow, Hiphil of ṣāmaḥ) — the pairing of qeren (horn = strength, power, dynasty) with ṣāmaḥ (to sprout, shoot up — agricultural growth verb) produces the "sprouting horn," a unique image. ṣemaḥ (a branch, shoot) will become the technical term for the Messiah in Jeremiah 23:5, Psalms 33:15; Zechariah 3:8, Psalms 6:12 — the Branch. nēr limsîḥî (a lamp for my anointed) — the lamp burning in the dynasty; cf. 1 Kings 11:36 ("that my servant David may always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem"). Luke 1:69 ("a horn of salvation in the house of his servant David") and Psalms 2:32 ("a light for revelation to the Gentiles") echo these images.
18: ʾôyĕbāyw ʾalbîš bōšet wĕʿālāyw yāṣîṣ nizrô — "I will clothe his enemies in shame, but his crown will gleam/blossom upon his head" — the contrast of garments: enemies clothed in bōšet (shame, disgrace) :: the anointed wearing a gleaming crown. yāṣîṣ nizrô (his crown/diadem will gleam/blossom, from ṣîṣ, to shine, glisten, blossom) — the nēzer (crown, diadem, consecration, from nāzar, to consecrate/separate) is the priestly-royal crown. The verb ṣîṣ captures both shining (like polished gold) and blossoming (like a flower) — the Messianic promise closes Ps 132 with an image of radiant, flourishing kingship that will not fade.
